name: FCC Capture
description: FCC Capture is a conspiracy theory alleging that the Federal Communications Commission (FCC), the independent agency of the United States government that regulates communications by radio, television, wire, satellite, and cable, is unduly influenced or controlled by the industries it is supposed to regulate. Proponents of this theory argue that this 'capture' leads to policies that favor the interests of large media conglomerates and telecommunications companies at the expense of the public interest, innovation, and fair competition. The theory often cites instances where FCC rulings appear to benefit specific companies or industries, deregulation efforts that concentrate media ownership, and the revolving door phenomenon where individuals move between the FCC and the industries it regulates. Critics of the FCC capture theory argue that while lobbying and industry influence are undeniable aspects of the political landscape, the FCC operates within a framework of laws, regulations, and oversight that prevents complete regulatory capture. They point to instances where the FCC has ruled against industry interests, enforced regulations, and promoted consumer protection. Mainstream scholars acknowledge the potential for regulatory capture in any industry but emphasize the importance of transparency, public participation, and independent oversight to mitigate its effects. They also note that the FCC's decisions are often subject to judicial review, providing another check on its power.
subtitle: Undue influence over communications regulation.

claims.claim: The FCC favors large media conglomerates.
claims.evidence: Deregulation of media ownership rules allowing companies to own more media outlets.
claims.criticism: Deregulation is argued to promote competition and innovation, and the FCC retains some restrictions on media ownership.

claims.claim: The 'revolving door' phenomenon compromises FCC independence.
claims.evidence: Former FCC commissioners and staff moving to positions in the industries they previously regulated.
claims.criticism: Individuals are entitled to pursue career opportunities, and their prior experience can be valuable in the private sector. Ethics rules also limit their influence immediately after leaving the FCC.

claims.claim: Net neutrality repeal was a result of FCC capture.
claims.evidence: The repeal of net neutrality rules was supported by large ISPs and opposed by consumer advocates.
claims.criticism: Arguments for repeal focused on promoting investment in broadband infrastructure and reducing regulatory burdens. The FCC argued net neutrality stifled innovation.

keyFigures.name: Robert McChesney
keyFigures.role: Media scholar and proponent of the theory.

keyFigures.name: Ajit Pai
keyFigures.role: Former FCC Chairman criticized for deregulation policies.

mythologyConnections: The concept of a powerful entity controlling information flow echoes themes in mythology.
The David and Goliath narrative, where an underdog struggles against a powerful force, resonates with this theory.

timeline.event: Creation of the Federal Communications Commission (FCC).
Telecommunications Act of 1996, leading to media consolidation.
FCC repeals net neutrality rules under Chairman Ajit Pai.

sources.author: Tim Wu
sources.work: The Master Switch: The Rise and Fall of Information Empires
sources.title: Tim Wu

sources.author: Eli Noam
sources.work: Media Ownership and Concentration in America
sources.title: Eli Noam

worshipPractices.name: Petitioning the Algorithm
worshipPractices.description: Followers meticulously craft online petitions and social media campaigns, believing that enough digital pressure can sway the FCC's decisions. They see the internet as a modern oracle, capable of influencing the seemingly immutable forces of regulatory power.

worshipPractices.name: The Annual Unplugging
worshipPractices.description: Once a year, devotees symbolically disconnect from all forms of regulated communication. This act represents a temporary rejection of the perceived control and a reaffirmation of individual autonomy over information.

festivals.name: Data Privacy Day Vigil
festivals.description: A solemn gathering where followers share personal stories of perceived overreach and lament the erosion of digital privacy. They light candles representing lost anonymity and vow to protect their data from capture.

festivals.name: The Bandwidth Bonfire
festivals.description: Participants gather to symbolically burn outdated technology and documents perceived as instruments of control. This ritual is intended to cleanse the community of negative influences and usher in a new era of open communication.

cultural.modernLegacy: The myth of FCC Capture fuels distrust in media and government institutions, contributing to the spread of misinformation and conspiracy theories. It also inspires advocacy for net neutrality and digital rights, influencing policy debates and shaping online activism.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Mimir, guarding the Well of Wisdom, parallels the FCC as a gatekeeper of information. Capture is analogous to Odin sacrificing his eye for a drink from the well, representing industry influence corrupting impartial regulation.

The Oracle, meant to provide unbiased prophecies, could be swayed with offerings. This mirrors how lobbying and industry relationships can influence the FCC's decisions, compromising its intended objectivity.

Ma'at represents cosmic order and justice. FCC capture disrupts this balance in the realm of communication, similar to how actions against Ma'at would upset the natural order, leading to chaos and imbalance in society.

Indra, king of the gods, is sometimes depicted as succumbing to temptations and losing sight of dharma (righteous conduct). FCC capture mirrors Indra's fallibility, where the agency's pursuit of its mission is compromised by external pressures and self-interest.

associations.name: Serpent
associations.significance: The serpent symbolizes hidden influence and the subtle manipulation of power from within, reflecting the idea of industry capture.

associations.name: Ivy
associations.significance: Ivy, known for clinging and suffocating, represents how industry can gradually take over and control a regulatory body.

associations.name: The Golden Fleece
associations.significance: The Golden Fleece embodies the pursuit of wealth and power, illustrating the intense competition of industries seeking to control the FCC for financial gain.

associations.name: Shadow
associations.significance: Shadow represents the lack of transparency and the hidden agendas that often accompany the alleged manipulation of regulatory bodies.

associations.name: Quicksand
associations.significance: Quicksand symbolizes the slow but inevitable sinking of the FCC's independence as it becomes increasingly entangled with industry interests.

associations.name: Lunar Eclipse
associations.significance: A lunar eclipse represents the obscuring of truth and clarity, suggesting that the public is being kept in the dark about the FCC's true allegiances.

associations.name: Three
associations.significance: Three represents a betrayal, where two entities (industry and a captured FCC) collude against a third (the public interest).

companions.name: Argus Panoptes
companions.description: Argus, with his hundred eyes, symbolizes the constant surveillance needed to fight against FCC capture and expose any undue influence. He represents vigilance against hidden agendas.

companions.name: Prometheus
companions.description: Prometheus, who defied the gods to bring fire (knowledge) to humanity, would be an ally in the fight to expose corruption within the FCC and empower the public with information. He represents the pursuit of truth against powerful forces.

companions.name: Hermes
companions.description: Hermes, the messenger god, is a swift and resourceful companion, essential for rapidly disseminating information about FCC actions and potential capture. He facilitates communication and transparency.

companions.name: The Erinyes (Furies)
companions.description: The Erinyes, goddesses of vengeance, would act as protectors, ensuring that those who manipulate the FCC for personal gain face consequences. They represent justice and retribution for corrupt actions.

companions.name: Gullinbursti
companions.description: Freyr's golden boar, Gullinbursti, illuminates the path forward, helping to cut through misinformation and uncover the truth about FCC operations. Its golden bristles shine a light on corruption.

extendedContent.title: Echoes of Ancient Power Dynamics: The Capture Archetype
extendedContent.content: The concept of 'capture,' wherein an entity meant to regulate or oversee is subverted and controlled by the very forces it is intended to monitor, resonates deeply with archetypal narratives found across world mythologies. Consider the dragon Fafnir from Norse mythology, initially a dwarf who guarded a vast hoard of gold. The gold, meant to be a form of security and wealth for the community, ultimately corrupted Fafnir, transforming him into a monstrous dragon obsessed with protecting it for himself. This mirrors the FCC capture theory, where the regulatory body (the 'hoard') becomes the possession of the industries it is meant to oversee (Fafnir). Similarly, in Greek mythology, the Titan Cronus devoured his own children, fearing they would usurp his power. This act represents a perversion of natural order, where a figure of authority (Cronus) abuses his position to maintain control, sacrificing the well-being of others. The FCC capture theory suggests a similar perversion, where the FCC, meant to protect the public interest, is allegedly subverted to serve the interests of powerful corporations. These mythological parallels highlight a fundamental human fear: that power, once entrusted, can be twisted and used against those it was meant to serve.

extendedContent.title: The Siren Song of Influence: Temptation and Regulatory Capture
extendedContent.content: Mythological narratives often explore the seductive nature of power and influence, highlighting how even well-intentioned individuals can succumb to temptation. The Sirens of Greek mythology, with their enchanting voices, lured sailors to their doom, representing the dangers of succumbing to irresistible allure. This can be seen as analogous to the ways in which industries might influence the FCC. The promise of lucrative post-government employment, campaign contributions, or favorable media coverage can act as a 'siren song', swaying regulators to make decisions that benefit the industry rather than the public. The story of Pandora's Box also offers a parallel. Pandora, despite being warned not to open the box, was overcome by curiosity and unleashed all the evils of the world. This illustrates the inherent danger of unchecked power and the potential for even seemingly innocuous actions to have devastating consequences. Regulatory capture can be seen as a modern-day Pandora's Box, where small concessions and compromises can lead to a systemic erosion of regulatory oversight and a concentration of power in the hands of a few.

extendedContent.title: The Labyrinth of Bureaucracy: Navigating Complexity and Corruption
extendedContent.content: Mythological labyrinths, like the one built by Daedalus for King Minos, symbolize the complexities and hidden dangers of bureaucratic systems. These labyrinths, designed to trap the Minotaur, also represent the potential for corruption and manipulation within intricate structures. The FCC, with its complex regulations and procedures, can be seen as a modern-day labyrinth. The FCC capture theory suggests that industries have learned to navigate this labyrinth, using lobbying, legal loopholes, and political connections to influence policy decisions. The story of the Gordian Knot, which Alexander the Great famously cut instead of untying, highlights the frustration and difficulty in resolving complex problems. The FCC capture theory posits that the system is so entrenched that a radical solution, akin to cutting the Gordian Knot, might be necessary to break the cycle of influence and restore regulatory integrity. The labyrinthine structure of regulatory bodies can inadvertently create opportunities for those seeking to exploit the system for their own gain, making it crucial to maintain transparency and accountability.

extendedContent.title: The Watchdogs Asleep: Cerberus and the Guardians Betrayed
extendedContent.content: In Greek mythology, Cerberus, the three-headed dog, guards the gates of the Underworld, preventing the dead from escaping and the living from entering without permission. Cerberus represents the ideal of a vigilant guardian, fiercely protecting its charge. The FCC, in its role as a regulatory body, is meant to act as a 'watchdog' for the public interest, safeguarding the airwaves and ensuring fair competition. The FCC capture theory suggests that this watchdog has fallen asleep, allowing industries to exploit loopholes and manipulate the system without consequence. This betrayal of trust echoes the story of Argus Panoptes, the hundred-eyed giant tasked with guarding Io. Argus was eventually lulled to sleep and killed, allowing Hermes to free Io. Similarly, the FCC capture theory suggests that regulators have been effectively 'lulled to sleep' by industry influence, allowing them to pursue their own agendas at the expense of the public good. The failure of these mythological guardians serves as a cautionary tale about the importance of maintaining vigilance and resisting the temptations of power.

extendedContent.title: The Price of Hubris: Icarus and the Consequences of Unfettered Ambition
extendedContent.content: The myth of Icarus, who flew too close to the sun with wings made of feathers and wax, serves as a cautionary tale about the dangers of hubris and unchecked ambition. Icarus, blinded by his newfound power of flight, ignored his father's warnings and soared too high, ultimately leading to his demise. This narrative resonates with the FCC capture theory in that it highlights the potential consequences of allowing industries to operate without proper oversight. When corporations are allowed to pursue their own interests without regard for the public good, they can become like Icarus, flying too close to the sun and ultimately causing harm to themselves and others. The story of Prometheus, who stole fire from the gods and was punished for his transgression, also illustrates the importance of respecting boundaries and the potential consequences of defying established rules. Regulatory capture can be seen as a form of 'stealing fire', where industries seize control of the regulatory process for their own benefit, ultimately undermining the integrity of the system and endangering the public good.
